coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots decorated N-doped ZnO: Synthesis and enhanced photocatalytic activity on UV, visible and daylight sources with suppressed photocorrosion
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Muthulingam, S.
Bae, Kang Bin
Khan, Rizwan
Lee, In-Hwan
Uthirakumar, Periyayya
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots decorated N-doped ZnO (CQD/N-ZnO) composites were prepared by a simple one step method. The existence of CQD and ZnO phases in the CQD/N-ZnO composite was confirmed by XRD, Raman, XPS and HRTEM techniques. The CQD/N-ZnO composite exhibited an enhanced photocatalytic performance on aqueous malachite green dye, irrespective to the light sources of UV, visible and daylight. The dye degradation of ∼38% and 99% was noticed for N-doped ZnO and CQD/N-ZnO photocatalyst, respectively after 15min of daylight irradiation and it required just 30min to degrade an entire dye. Repeated photocatalytic experiment was carried out to investigate the reusing capability of CQD/N-ZnO photocatalyst and it was found that the suppressed photocorrosion offered by CQDs coverage on the ZnO crystals.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Zinc oxide
Malachite green
Photocatalyst
Photocorrosion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Confirmation of N-doping by XPS studies
Scanning and transmission electron microscopy analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of (a) N-doped ZnO and (b) CQD/N-ZnO composites.
The XPS spectra of CQD/N-ZnO composites with (a) full survey scan and high resolution core (b) N 1s, (c) Zn 2p, and (d) O 1s scans.
Raman spectra of (a) N-doped ZnO and (b) CQD/N-ZnO composites.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra and (b) the band gap calculation of N-doped ZnO and CQD/N-ZnO composites.

FESEM images of (a) N-doped ZnO and (b) CQD/N-ZnO composites. TEM image of (c) N-doped ZnO, (d) CQDs, and (e) CQD/N-ZnO. (f) HRTEM image of CQD/N-ZnO composites and the insets are the lattice space values of carbon and ZnO.

Photodegradation of (a) pure MG dye (without catalyst), (b) N-doped ZnO and (c) CQD/N-ZnO photocatalysts under daylight irradiation [insets: the respective photographs of the MG dye solution taken after regular intervals (0–60min)]. (d) The comparative photocatalytic profile of MG dye with respect to different photocatalysts under daylight irradiation.

(a) The comparative photodegradation profile and (b) their respective photographs of MG dye under different light sources. The UV–vis absorption spectra of MG dye degradation in presence of CQD/N-ZnO under (c) daylight and (d) UV-light irradiation.
Photostability graphs of (a) different photocatalysts under daylight irradiation in three cycle runs. (b) Fourth cycle performance of different photocatalysts under daylight irradiation.
TEM images of (a) CQD/N-ZnO and (b) N-doped ZnO photocatalysts along with their (c) XRD patterns after fourth cycle.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots decorated N-doped ZnO: Synthesis and enhanced photocatalytic activity on UV, visible and daylight sources with suppressed photocorrosion

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanoscience Center for Optoelectronic and Energy Devices [Nano-COED], Department of Chemistry, Sona College of Technology, Salem, Tamil Nadu 636 005, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanoscience Center for Optoelectronic and Energy Devices [Nano-COED]

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Advanced Materials Engineering and Research Center of Advanced Materials Development, Chonbuk National University, Jeonju 561-756, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Advanced Materials Engineering and Research Center of Advanced Materials Development

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Advanced Materials Engineering and Research Center of Advanced Materials Development, Chonbuk National University, Jeonju 561-756, South Korea.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly efficient and eco-friendly CQD/N-ZnO composite exhibits an improved photocatalytic activity under different light sources.

Carbon quantum dots decorated N-doped ZnO (CQD/N-ZnO) composites were prepared by a simple one step method. The existence of CQD and ZnO phases in the CQD/N-ZnO composite was confirmed by XRD, Raman, XPS and HRTEM techniques. The CQD/N-ZnO composite exhibited an enhanced photocatalytic performance on aqueous malachite green dye, irrespective to the light sources of UV, visible and daylight. The dye degradation of ∼38% and 99% was noticed for N-doped ZnO and CQD/N-ZnO photocatalyst, respectively after 15min of daylight irradiation and it required just 30min to degrade an entire dye. Repeated photocatalytic experiment was carried out to investigate the reusing capability of CQD/N-ZnO photocatalyst and it was found that the suppressed photocorrosion offered by CQDs coverage on the ZnO crystals.

ce:section.ce:para: The progress towards the development of efficient photocatalyst is a critical issue for an environmental remediation due to major organic contaminants originated from the textile and food industries. The malachite green (MG) dye is largely used to color in silk, wool and leather industries and it is extremely toxic to fish [1,2]. The MG dye is easily entering into the environment from municipal wastewater treatment plants and it is suspected of being capable of causing gene damage and causing cancer [2]. Now a days, it becomes one of the challenging tasks to eradicate the MG dye in the wastewater. Hence, various inorganic semiconductors are recommended to use as a photocatalyst to decrease the environmental impacts due to its efficiency and broad applicability. Amongst, zinc oxide (ZnO) is blooming nowadays as of being its high catalytic activity, low cost, and environmental friendliness over other photocatalysts [3]. However, a quick recombination and the narrow absorption band of the ZnO photocatalyst are the major concerns that affect their photocatalytic efficiency. To overwhelm these issues, a suitable modification is obligatory for developing new types of ZnO nanostructures.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have received greater attention because of their advantages in non-toxic, photostability, low cost, heavy metal-free and eco-friendly over traditional toxic heavy-metals based quantum dots creating serious health and environmental issues [4]. There are many reports related to carbon related materials were found in the literature [5–7]. However, it was rare work involved to prepare ZnO photocatalyst covered with carbon dots [3]. The usage of CQDs are being acknowledged due to the characteristic photo-induced electron transfer [7] and up-conversion of photoluminescence properties [3]. Hence, a new type of photocatalyst, CQD decorated N-doped ZnO (CQD/N-ZnO) was prepared for higher photocatalytic performance due to the combinational effect of N-doping [8] together with the carbon nanoparticle [5–13]. The combination of N-doped ZnO and CQDs seems to be an ideal tactic for endorsing the improvement in charge separation by hindering charge recombination to enhance the photocatalytic efficiency and anti-photocorrosion.

In addition, the new type of photocatalysts should enable us to use under different light sources of UV, visible and daylight. Herein, we report an easy methodology for preparing N-doped ZnO, CQDs, and CQD/N-ZnO composites, exhibiting an enhanced photocatalytic activity on degrading MG dye under UV, visible and daylight irradiation.

All the chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Typically for synthesizing N-doped ZnO, about 1.25g of zinc acetate dihydrate was dissolved completely in 125ml of double distilled water. The aqueous ammonia solution was added slowly to keep the solution pH to be around 10. The resultant clear solution was filtered and it was further mixed with 50ml of double distilled water and kept at 60°C on a constant temperature bath for 26h. White crystals of N-ZnO photocatalyst were collected, washed with distilled water and ethanol several times and dried [14].

A slightly modified Hummer’s method was used to prepare CQDs. Briefly, 50mg of carbon black pigment (HIBLACK Orion) was put into the mixture of 13ml H2SO4 and 6.5ml HNO3 at room temperature and sonicated for few minutes for better dispersion. The reaction mixture was maintained at 240°C for 2h and it was allowed to cool naturally to room temperature. Then, the reaction mixture was neutralized with sodium hydroxide (1M) solution at room temperature and excess salts were removed by dialysis for 2 days to isolated CQDs. The average particle size of CQD was about 2.5nm.

For CQD/N-ZnO photocatalyst was prepared as follows: a required amount (10μl) of CQD solution was mixed with 50mg of ZnO crystals dispersed in 25ml of ethanol and stirred for 30min at room temperature. The CQD/N-ZnO photocatalysts were removed by filtration, washed with ethanol several times and dried for further characterization.

5mg of photocatalyst was suspended in 100ml of aqueous MG dye solution (1.0×10−4 M) taken in a glass beaker and agitated in the absence of light for 30min to establish an absorption–desorption equilibrium. After a given UV irradiation (Philips, 18W TLD), about 5ml of the mixture was withdrawn and the photocatalysts were separated by centrifuging (7000rpm; 10min) in prior to measure the photodegradation. In general, the absorption spectrum of MG dye consists of three peaks, a major peak was positioning at 612nm and other two minor peaks were at 418 and 310nm. In order to monitor the degradation characteristic of MG dye, absorption peak appeared at 612nm was considered rather than the other minor peaks, for accuracy. The similar photocatalytic experiments were carried out under a visible light (Philips, 18W CFL) and direct daylight source to investigate the impact of degradation trend under different light sources. The daylight intensity (33×103 ±5000lx) was measured by digital lux-meter during October to December 2014 (11am–2pm). All the experiments were undertaken in triplicate with errors below 4% and average values were reported.

The powder X-ray diffractograms (XRD) patterns of the samples were recorded by a Rigaku using Cu Kα radiation (λ =0.1546nm) monochromated by a nickel filter. The surface electronic states of the photocatalyst were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS Ultra DLD (Kratos Inc.), monochromatic Al Kα radiation (1486.6eV, 150W). Raman spectra were collected on Raman spectroscopy (nano-Raman, NTEGRA) equipped with a synapse CCD detector and a confocal Olympus microscope. Morphology was investigated by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, SUPRA-40VP equipped with an EDXS system) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL-2010). Photocatalytic activity measurement was analyzed with a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2550, Shimadzu).

The crystal structure and phase composition of N-doped ZnO and CQD/N-ZnO composites were revealed by X-ray diffraction analysis. Fig. 1 shows the XRD patterns of N-doped ZnO and CQD/N-ZnO composites. The observed X-ray diffraction peaks were perfectly indexed to the hexagonal phase ZnO (JCPDS No. 79-0206) and the primary diffraction peaks appeared at 2θ values of 31.7°, 33.4° and 36.2°, representing (100), (002) and (101) assigned crystal planes of ZnO [14]. While for the CQD/N-ZnO composite, notably, a broader diffraction peak at near 26° attributing to graphitic (002) peak evidently appears together with regular characteristic ZnO diffraction peaks, indicating that the CQDs were successfully coated on ZnO surface [9].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Confirmation of N-doping by XPS studies
ce:section.ce:para: X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was performed to examine the chemical state and surface composition of powder samples to confirm the nitrogen doping in ZnO crystals. Fig. 2 shows the XPS survey scan and the high-resolution XPS spectra of core level Zn 2p, N 1s, and O 1s of CQD/N-ZnO composites. The full XPS survey scan of CQD/N-ZnO (Fig. 2a) exhibits the distinctive peaks that were assigned to Zn, O, C and N. The core level high resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2b) confirms the nitrogen doping with the appearance of characteristic peaks at 396.1, 397.2, 398.8, and 400.1eV. The major peak appeared at 396.1eV was assigned to NZnN bond and the peak at 397.2eV was assigned to NC bond [15]. The other two peaks at 398.8 and 400.1eV were allocated to NH bond and OZnN, respectively [15,16]. The detailed high resolution XPS spectra of Zn 2p, and O 1s core level scans can be seen in Fig. 2c and d .

The nano-Raman spectroscopy was performed to check the occurrence of Raman-active modes of CQD and ZnO phases in CQD/N-ZnO composite as shown in Fig. 3 a. The Raman-active modes of ZnO at 331, 434, and 580cm−1 were related to the second-order scattering arising from zone-boundary phonons 2E2, E2 (high) and the polar symmetry modes A1 (LO) [17], respectively. Similarly, the occurrence of carbon related Raman-active modes of graphite (G) and defect (D) bands were observed at 1340 and 1600cm−1, respectively, suggesting the presence of CQD and ZnO related Raman active modes in CQD/N-ZnO composite, when compared to the N-doped ZnO.

Furthermore, the UV–vis absorption spectra of N-doped ZnO and CQD/N-ZnO composite were measured as shown in Fig. 4 . The representative absorption peak with a transition in the UV region was observed in both samples. However, CQD/N-ZnO composite shows an extended wide absorption ranging from UV to visible region (up to 600nm) together with a slightly lower bandgap value at 3.1eV from 3.21eV, when compared with N-doped ZnO (Fig. 4b). Thus, the UV results provide an additional support that CQDs were successfully decorated on ZnO crystals, which is in agreement with the XRD and Raman results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scanning and transmission electron microscopy analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The high magnification FESEM images of N-doped ZnO and CQD/N-ZnO composites are displayed in Fig. 5 a and b. The size and shape of N-doped ZnO seem to be spherical shaped nanoparticles (∼25nm), and it remains unchanged after coverage of CQDs in CQD/N-ZnO photocatalyst as shown in Fig. 5b. Moreover, it is very hard to visualize the changes in size of ZnO after CQD coverage in FESEM images. Hence, the TEM images of N-doped ZnO, CQDs and CQD/N-ZnO composites are presented in Fig. 5c–f . The particle size of N-doped ZnO crystal and pure CQD were found to be ∼25 and 2.5nm, respectively. The insets in Fig. 6 f show the HRTEM image of CQD/N-ZnO composites and the lattice spacing were measured to be ∼0.26nm for ZnO and ∼0.32nm for carbon [9]. These results were strongly authenticate the existence of CQDs and ZnO phases in CQD/N-ZnO photocatalyst.

Fig. 6 shows the photocatalytic activity of the N-doped ZnO and CQD/N-ZnO composites on MG dye under daylight source. Fig. 6a shows the ∼18% of pure MG dye degradation after 60min of daylight irradiation without photocatalyst. However, the degradation efficiency was improved significantly in presence of N-doped ZnO (∼60%) and CQD/N-ZnO photocatalysts (∼100%) after 30min of daylight irradiation. This observation was further confirmed with the photographs of MG dye solution taken after regular intervals of 0–60min of daylight irradiation, displayed as an inset. The respective UV–vis absorption spectra of MG dye degradation with respect to different daylight exposure in presence of N-doped ZnO and CQD/N-ZnO photocatalysts are displayed in Fig. 6b and c. Fig. 6d shows the comparative degradation efficiency graph of pure MG dye, N-doped ZnO and CQD/N-ZnO composites under daylight irradiation. These results demonstrated that CQD/N-ZnO composite exhibit an enhanced photocatalytic performance and it required just 30min to degrade entire MG dye under daylight irradiation.

The photocatalytic performance of CQD/N-ZnO composites was further interrelated with other common light sources of UV and visible light irradiation as shown in Fig. 7 . Irrespective of light sources, CQD/N-ZnO composites degrades an entire dye within 30min of light irradiation. However, as seen in Fig. 7a that a slight disparity in the percentage of degradation was noticed after 15min of light exposure of UV (90%), visible (95%), and daylight sources (99%). These results were consistent with the photographs (Fig. 7b) of the respective dye solution taken after regular intervals of 15min of light irradiation. Besides, a slight blue shade was still appears in both UV and visible light irradiated samples (after 15min), whereas, no such noticeable shade was detected in daylight irradiated dye solution. Thus, we concluded that the CQD/N-ZnO photocatalyst shows an enhanced photodegradation, regardless to the light sources.

To validate the photocatalytic performance of CQD/N-ZnO composites, photodegradation percentage of CQD/N-ZnO composite was compared with other recent reports related to the CQD covered metal oxide photocatalysts. For instance, CQD/ZnO heterostructure disclosed ∼80% degradation of RhB dye even after 2h of UV light irradiation [18]. The CQD/α-Fe2O3 photocatalyst destroys methylene blue [MB] dye in 90min by adding H2O2 under visible light [19]. Also, ZnO/graphene photocatalyst shows around 90% degradation of MB dye in 40min under UV light [20]. Similarly, TiO2/C-dots photocatalyst needs at least 4h to degrade MB dye under visible light [15], and just 90% degradation of MB dye was resulted in 4h under near IR light with CQDs/Cu2O [21]. Therefore, based on these information, we concluded that an enhanced photocatalytic performance of CQD/N-ZnO composite primarily from the combinational effect of both CQD and N-doping characteristics and the possible photocatalytic reaction were summarized.
(1) ZnO/CQDs+ hν →e−(CQDs)+h+(ZnO)
(2) e− +O2 →O2 −
(3) h+ +OH− →OH
(4) O2
− +H2O→HO2
+OH−
(5) HO2
+H2O→H2O2 +OH
(6) H2O2 →2OH
(7) OH +dye→CO2 +H2O
(8) HO2
+dye→CO2 +H2O
(9) O2
− +dye→CO2 +H2O

In order to demonstrate the suppressed photocorrosion, the repeated experiments were conducted with N-doped ZnO and CQD/N-ZnO photocatalysts and results indicated that CQD/N-ZnO composites was a relatively stable photocatalyst to be reused as an effective daylight photocatalyst. Fig. 8 shows the successive experimental results of N-doped ZnO and CQD/N-ZnO composites with three cycles under daylight irradiation of 60min. The photocatalytic efficiency of N-doped ZnO decreased significantly from 83% (cycle 1) to 60% (cycle 3), while relatively lesser decreasing trend was observed in CQD/N-ZnO composites (100–91%). The reduction in the degradation efficiency of CQD/N-ZnO composites probably due to the mass loss percent of photocatalyst during each cycle. The expected mass loss is lower than 2–3% by weight per cycle. Furthermore, fourth cycle experiment was performed to investigate the reusing capacity of photocatalysts. In Fig. 8b, it was found that CQD/N-ZnO composite required just 90min to degrade the MG dye completely beyond weight loses, while N-doped ZnO proceeded with 4.2h to degrade around 90% of MG dye under daylight irradiation. This result strongly authenticates the suppressed photocorrosion offered by the CQDs, which could effectively inhibits the photocorrosion of ZnO crystals.

Fig. 9 shows the TEM images and XRD spectra of fourth successive photocatalytic performance of N-doped ZnO and CQD/N-ZnO photocatalysts. The morphology of CQD/N-ZnO photocatalyst was remain unchanged with a slight variation in the shape of the particles after fourth successive experiment. However, wire-like morphologies were observed together with the regular ZnO particles in case of N-doped ZnO photocatalyst (Fig. 9b), due to photocorrosion of ZnO crystals. This statement was further substantiated with XRD results as shown in Fig. 9c . For instance, in case of N-doped ZnO, the appearance of new X-ray diffraction peaks at 27.6°, 32.7°, 59.3° and 60.3° representing the formation of Zn5(OH)6(CO3)2 phase of ZnO crystals. While, the CQD/N-ZnO photocatalyst displays their characteristic ZnO diffraction peaks. This observation strongly confirmed that the coverage of CQDs could be act as a shield to preserve the ZnO crystals away from the photocorrosion.

A simple method was demonstrated to prepare CQD/N-doped ZnO as an effective photocatalyst. Existence of both CQD and ZnO phases in CQD/N-ZnO photocatalyst was confirmed by using various techniques. Photocatalytic response of CQD/N-ZnO photocatalyst on the aqueous MG dye was investigated under daylight irradiation and results were found to be consistent with other common light sources of UV and visible lights. It was recognized that a wider range of light excitation capability of CQD/N-ZnO can facilitate the higher separation efficiency along with the suppressed photocorrosion, leading to suggest as a promising photocatalyst for wastewater treatment in industry.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was encouraged by the management of Sona College of Technology and supported by National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning (2013R1A2A2A07067688, 2010-0019626).
